Fern Polrrrcnl PRACrrcEs CouvrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramenro, CA 95914-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
May 20,201O
Maggie Houlihan
Encinitas City Council
City of Encinitas
505 South Vulcan Avenue
Encinitas, CA92024
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. ^{-10-077
Dear Ms. Houlihan:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").' This letter is based on the facts piesented. The Fair political
Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby
(1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTION
Are payments for your participation in a "cultural exchange" in [stanbul, Turkey,
including but not limited to your transportation while in Turkey, iccommodations, meals, and
museum visits, prohibited gifts under the Act?
CONCLUSION
Payments for your travel, including your transportation while in Turkey and related
lodging and subsistence, are gifts under the Act and must be reported on your annual statement
of economic interest. As reportable gifts, these payments may also requiie you to disqualify
yourself from governmental decisions affecting the source of the gifts. Notwithstanding the fact
that these payments are reportable, if the payments are made by the Pacifica Institute, o SOt("X:)
nonprofit organization, they are not subject to the Act's $420 gift limit.
Apart from payments for travel such as transportation and related lodging and
subsistence, payments for your activities while in Turkey may also be gifts urd". the Act.
' The Political Reform Act is contained in GovernmentCode Sections 81000 through gl0l4. All statLrkrry
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the r'-air political practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through ltt997 of Tirle 2 of thi Calitirrnia Code of Regulati.ns. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-lO-077
Page No. 2
However, these payments must be examined on a case-by-case basis under the Act's applicable
gift mles. For activities such as tours, payments are reportable gifts and subject to the Act's
$420 gift limit unless the tour is considered informational material as discusied below.
Additionally, your pro-rata share of the cost of an invitation-only event is reportable and subject
to the $420 gift limit, except that payments for subsistence such as food and beverages provided
at the event are not counted towards the gift limit if paid for by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit
organization or other individual or entity specified in Section g9506(a)(2).
FACTS
You are a city council member for the City of Encinitas and have been invited by the
Pacifica Institute San Diego Branch, a nonprofit organization under Section 501(cX3) oi the
Internal Revenue Code, to a "cultural exchange" event in Istanbul, Turkey. As you have
described the event, it "is aimed at intense cultural immersions involving leading govemment
and city officials, faith community leaders, and academicians living throughout Southern
California'" The event will last approximately ten days, and participants *itt experience major
historical sites throughout Turkey. In addition, participants will "meet and confer,'for
"informational exchanges" as they visit such places as educational institutions, hospitals, a major
television station, and a daily newspaper operation.
All costs for your participation in the event, excluding round trip air travel, will be
covered by the Pacifica Institute through its collected donations and sponsors in Turkey.
Covered expenses potentially include accommodations, transportation within Turkey, meals, and
museum visits.
AIIALYSIS
The term "gift" is defined in Section 82028(a) as:
"Any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the
extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is
made in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to
official status."2
In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials,3 the Act regulates the
receipt of gifts by local public officials in three ways that are relevant to your question.
I Section 82044 defines payment, in part, as any ..rendering of
whether tangible or intangible."
I A "public otficial" is "every member, offlcer, employee, or consultant of a state or local governmental
agency." (Section 82048.)
. services or anything else of value,


File No. A-10-077
Page No. 3
First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials. As
a city council member, you are prohibited from accepting gifts from any single source in any
calendar year with a total value of more than the gift limit. (Section 89503(a).) The current gift
limit, which is adjusted biennially each odd number year to reflect changes in the Consumer
Price lndex, is $420. (Regulation 18940.2.)
Secondly, so that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the Act
imposes reporting obligations requiring certain public officials to disclose the source of any gifts
aggregating $50 or more in value. Section 87203 provides that "[e]very person who holds an
office specified in Section 87200 shall . . . file a statement disclosing his [or her.l investments, his
[or her] interests in real property and his [or her] income . . .." The term "income" includes any
gift. (Section 82030.) As a city council member, you hold an office specified in Section 87200.
Accordingly, you must report the name and address of each source of gifts aggregating $50 or
more in value. (Section 87207(a)(l).)
Finally, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
using his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the
donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $420 or more provided to, received by, or
promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. (Sections
87100, 87103(e), Regulations 18700, 18703.4.)
Notwithstanding the general gift provisions explained above, Section 89506 controls
whether a gift of travel is subject to the Act's gift limit providing the following in pertinent part:
"(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that are reasonably related to a
legislative or govemmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or
intemational public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if either of
the following apply:
tflI
"(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a
foreign government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private
educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation
Code, a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under Section
501(cX3) of the lnternal Revenue Code, or a by a person domiciled outside the
United States which substantially satisfies the requirements for tax exempt status
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Intemal Revenue Code."
Based upon the facts you have provided, your travel to Turkey appears to be reasonably
related to an issue of international public policy in that the travel will give you a historical and
informational perspective on the country and issues related to the country. So long as the source
of any payment for your travel is the Pacifica lnstitute, which is a non-profit organization exempt


File No. A-10-077
Page No.4
from taxation under Section 501(cX3) of the Internal Revenue Code, the payment is not subject
to the Act's $420 gift limit.a
Please note, however, that the exception to the $420 gift limit only applies to the costs of
travel such as transportation and related lodging and subsistence paid by ihe iacifica tnstitute. It
does not apply to payments for activities during the trip that do not conititute transportation,
lodging, or subsistence.s
In this regard, the itinerary you have provided includes activities that do not appear to fall
within the Act's applicable gift exceptions and would therefore be both reportable and subject to
the $420 gift limit. However, we do not have full descriptions of the activities planned or
information regarding the costs of these activities, other than the costs of travel such as
transportation, lodging, or subsistence. Thus, we can provide only general assistance regarding
those types of activities that fall within an exception to the Act's gift provisions and those typei
of activities which do not.
Tours and Site-Seeing
Regulation 189a2@)(L) excludes "informational materials" from the definition of a gift.
Regulation 18942.1defines "informational material" as "any item which serves primarily to-
convey information and which is provided to an official for the purpose of assisting him or her in
the performance of his or her official duties." The acquisition of adequate information is
essential to sound legislative and administrative action. It is not the purpose of the political
Reform Act to interfere in any way in the free flow of information to ofiicials. Only financial
pressures and inducements are sought to be limited and disclosed. (In re Spellman (1975) L
FPPC Ops. 16.)
Under certain circumstances, the Commission has interpreted "informational material" to
include informational tours such as on-site demonstrations and inspections designed specifically
for public officials. (See /n re Spellman, sl,pra, and Briggs Advici Letter, t{o. e-gf-itO.)
a While a payment for travel by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organizarion is not subject to the $420 gift limit, any
payment must be disclosed as a reportable gift under Section 87203. Moreover, the piyment may bar you from
making, participating in making, or influencing decisions atfecting the Pacifica Instiiute, the souice ofihe gift. Even
if a gift of travel from a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization is exempr from rhe $420 gift limit, you musr srill disqualify
yourself from any governmental decision with a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the source bf a 
-
giti or gifts with an aggregate value of $420 or more in the l2-month period prior to your participation in the
government decision. (Section 87lOO et seq.)
5 For example, we have previously determined that a payment tbr an oft'icial's tour of a historical
battleship, while traveling in France, was a gift subject to rhe Act's gift limit if paid for by the nonprofit organization
paying for the official's travel to France, because the tour did not app.o. to be ttr the purpose ofaisisting the
ofl'icial in the pertbrmance of her otTicial duties and therefore did not tall within tne ect's gift exception for
informational material. Similarly, we also determined that a payment fbr the otTicial's attendance al a private
concert not open to the general public was also a gifi subject to the Act's gift limit if paid for by the nonprofit
organization paying for the otTicial's travel and must be valued as an invitation-only event under Regulaiion
18946.2. (Fetlerighi Advise Letrer, No. A-}1--139.)


File No. A-10-077
Page No. 5
However, we have looked at several factors when determining whether a tour is indeed
informational material, including whether the tour will primarily convey information, whether
the tour is specifically designed for public officials, and whether the tour is formally structured to
convey information to the officials. Additionally, we consider whether the tour appears
extravagant in value or nature in relationship to the information conveyed. (Barker Advice
Letter, No. t-93-186.) In sum, a tour is informational material only if it is for the purpose of
assisting officials in the performance of their official duties. (See Federtgfti Advice Letter,
supra.)
According to the itinerary you have provided, the vast majority of the activities described
are tours of historical and cultural significance relating to Turkey and its various regions.
However, payments made for an official's tour do not fall within the exception to the Act's gift
limits for informational material merely because it can be said that the tour offers a historical or
cultural perspective of a country or region. For the most part, these tours appear to be
recreational in nature, and any relationship between the tours and your official duties with the
Encinitas City Council is tenuous at best. Thus, payments made for these activities (other than
travel payments for your transportation, lodging, or subsistence) are gifts subject to the $420 gift
limit, as well as reporting. As examples, payments made for admission to a museum or other
historical site, a tour guide, or other similar recreational activities are gifts subject to the $420
gift limit barring additional facts more closely tying the payments to your duties as a city council
member.
Nonetheless, your planned activities while in Turkey also include visits to educational
institutions, hospitals, and media facilities. These activities appear to serve a purpose more
closely tied to your duties as a city council member, and are not the types of activities commonly
associated with a recreational tourist. Thus, payments relating to these activities are exempt
from the Act's gift provisions as informational material under Regulation LBg42.
Organized Meals
The itinerary provided lists numerous organized meals during the course of the trip.6
While the Act's exception for travel paid for by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization covers
subsistence such as meals and beverages, payments made for activities arranged in coordination
with a meal do not fall within the exception for travel paid for by a nonprofit organization merely
because the activities occur simultaneously with the meal. For example, an invitation-only euer,i
such as a banquet, party, gala, celebration, or other similar ftrnction must be valued as provided
for by Regulation 18946.2, which states the following in pertinent part:
6 You have identified at least one meal that will be provided in the home of a local Turkish family. We
note that Regulation 18942(a)(7) provides an exception to the Act's gifi rules fbr..home hospitality,.'which is
defined as "the cost of hospitality (including fbod, beverages, or occasional lodging) provided to an official by an
individual in the individual's home when the individual is present" so long as the costs are not paid for by another
person, no person deducts the costs as a business expense on any government tax return, and there is no
understanding between the individual extending the hospitality and another person that compensation received by
the individual from the person includes a portion to be utilized to provide gitts of hospitality.


File No. A-lO-077
Page No.6
"(b) lnvitation-Only Events. ...when an official or candidate
attends an invitation-only event such as a banquet, party, gala, celebration,
or other similar ftrnction, other than a nonprofit or political ftrndraiser as
set forth in Regulation 18946.4, the value received is the official's or
candidate's pro rata share of the cost of the event, plus the value of any
specific item that is presented to the official or candidate at the event.
"(c) Pro rata share of the cost of the event. The term .pro rata
share of the cost of the event' means the cost of all food and beverages,
rent of the facilities, decorations, entertainment, and all other costs
associated with the event, divided by the number of acceptances or the
number of attendees."
Pursuant to Regulation 18946.2. if any individual or entity other than one as
specified in Section 89506(aX2) pays for an invitation-only event, the pro rata cost of the
event is a gift to you and subject to the $420 gift limit. Nonetheless, subsistence such as
a meal and beverages provided at an invitation-only event paid for by a 501(c)(3)
nonprofit organization or other individual or entity specified in Section 89506(aX2),
while in Turkey, are not subject to the Act's $420 gift limit. Thus, for an invitation-only
event paid for by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization, only the remainder of the pro-rata
share of the cost of the event excluding the value of the meal and beverages is subject to
the $420 gift limit. However, even if a meal and beverages are providedly a 501(c)(3)
nonprofit organization or other individual or entity specified in Section 89506(a)(2), your
full pro-rata share of the event must be reported and may potentially disqualify you fiom
taking part in decisions affecting the source of the payment.
Source of Gift
You have indicated that payments for the "cultural exchange" will come from the
Pacifica Institute's donors and sponsors. Thus, you must determine whether the source of any
particular payment is the Pacifica Institute or the donor or sponsor making a payment to the
Pacifica Institute for the event. Recently adopted Regulation 18945 prouid"r the Commission's
rule for determining the source of a gift and provides the following, in pertinent part:
"(a) General Rule. A person is the source of a gift if the person makes a
gift to an official and is not acting as an intermediary.
"(b) Gifts through an Intermediary.
"(l) A person is the source of a gift, and a third party is an intermediary of
the gift under Sections 87210 or 87313, if the person makes a payment to the third
party and the payment is used directly or indirectly by the third party to make a
gift to an official under any of the following conclitions:


File No. A-lO-077
Page No. 7
"(A) The person directs and controls the payment at the time it is used by
the third party to make a gift to the official.
"(B) The person and third party have agreed that the payment rvill be used
by the third party to make a gift to the official.
"(C) The person identifie
beneficiary of the payment prior
official.
s the official to the third party as the intended
to the third party making the payment to the
"(D) The third party identifies the official to the person as the intended
beneficiary of the payment prior to the person making the payment to rhe third
party.
"(E) The person knows or has reason to know that the sole or primary
purpose of the payment is to make gifts to officials. 
r
"(F) The official or the official's agent solicits the payment from the
person to the third party for the purpose of making a gift to the official."
Notwithstanding the rule for determining the source of a gift in Regulation 1g945(b)(2),
subdivision (d) provides:
"(d) Presumption of Source by Officials. An official may presllme that the
person delivering the gift or, if the gift is offered but has not been delivered, the
person offering the gift to him or her is the source of the gift unless any of the
following apply:
"(1) The person delivering or offering the gift discloses to the official the
actual source of the gift.
"(2) It is clear from the surrounding circumstances at the time the gift is
delivered or offered that the person delivering or offering the gift is not the actual
soLlrce of the gift.
"(3) The official solicits a payment pursuant to subdivision (bXlXF) and
receives or is offered a gift responsive to the solicitation within l2 months of
making the solicitation.
While you have indicated that payments for the "cultural exchange" will be made by the
donors and sponsors to the Pacifica [nstitute, you have not provided any-indication that the
payments made to the Pacitica Institute were in any way earmarked foian oflicial. Signiticantly,
the Pacifica Institttte has extended invitations to the "cultural exchange" to faith comrrurnity


File No. A-10-077
Page No. 8
leaders and academicians, as well as govemment officials. From these facts alone, you have no
reason to suspect that that the Pacifica Institute is not the actual sollrce of the gift. provided that
you did not solicit payments to the Pacifica Institute and barring additional information
indicating that the Pacifica Institute is acting as an intermediary for the payments pursuant to
Regulation 18945(b), you may assume the Pacifica Institute is the source of any payments for the
"cultural exchange."
lf you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (9L6) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
BL:jgl

Are payments for your participation in a "cultural exchange" in [stanbul, Turkey,
including but not limited to your transportation while in Turkey, iccommodations, meals, and
museum visits, prohibited gifts under the Act?

nonprofit organization, they are not subject to the Act's $420 gift limit.
Apart from payments for travel such as transportation and related lodging and
subsistence, payments for your activities while in Turkey may also be gifts urd". the Act.

regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

However, these payments must be examined on a case-by-case basis under the Act's applicable

Covered expenses potentially include accommodations, transportation within Turkey, meals, and

"Any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the
extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is

official status."2
In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials,3 the Act regulates the

whether tangible or intangible."

agency." (Section 82048.)
. services or anything else of value,
First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials. As

gift. (Section 82030.) As a city council member, you hold an office specified in Section 87200.
Accordingly, you must report the name and address of each source of gifts aggregating $50 or
more in value. (Section 87207(a)(l).)
Finally, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
using his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the

promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. (Sections

"(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that are reasonably related to a
legislative or govemmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or
intemational public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if either of
the following apply:
tflI
"(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a
foreign government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private
educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation
Code, a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under Section
501(cX3) of the lnternal Revenue Code, or a by a person domiciled outside the
United States which substantially satisfies the requirements for tax exempt status
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Intemal Revenue Code."
Based upon the facts you have provided, your travel to Turkey appears to be reasonably
related to an issue of international public policy in that the travel will give you a historical and
informational perspective on the country and issues related to the country. So long as the source
of any payment for your travel is the Pacifica lnstitute, which is a non-profit organization exempt
from taxation under Section 501(cX3) of the Internal Revenue Code, the payment is not subject

Please note, however, that the exception to the $420 gift limit only applies to the costs of

does not apply to payments for activities during the trip that do not conititute transportation,
lodging, or subsistence.s
In this regard, the itinerary you have provided includes activities that do not appear to fall
within the Act's applicable gift exceptions and would therefore be both reportable and subject to
the $420 gift limit. However, we do not have full descriptions of the activities planned or
information regarding the costs of these activities, other than the costs of travel such as
transportation, lodging, or subsistence. Thus, we can provide only general assistance regarding
those types of activities that fall within an exception to the Act's gift provisions and those typei
of activities which do not.
Tours and Site-Seeing
Regulation 189a2@)(L) excludes "informational materials" from the definition of a gift.
Regulation 18942.1defines "informational material" as "any item which serves primarily to-
convey information and which is provided to an official for the purpose of assisting him or her in
the performance of his or her official duties." The acquisition of adequate information is
essential to sound legislative and administrative action. It is not the purpose of the political
Reform Act to interfere in any way in the free flow of information to ofiicials. Only financial
pressures and inducements are sought to be limited and disclosed. (In re Spellman (1975) L
FPPC Ops. 16.)
Under certain circumstances, the Commission has interpreted "informational material" to
include informational tours such as on-site demonstrations and inspections designed specifically
for public officials. (See /n re Spellman, sl,pra, and Briggs Advici Letter, t{o. e-gf-itO.)

government decision. (Section 87lOO et seq.)
5 For example, we have previously determined that a payment tbr an oft'icial's tour of a historical
battleship, while traveling in France, was a gift subject to rhe Act's gift limit if paid for by the nonprofit organization
paying for the official's travel to France, because the tour did not app.o. to be ttr the purpose ofaisisting the
ofl'icial in the pertbrmance of her otTicial duties and therefore did not tall within tne ect's gift exception for
informational material. Similarly, we also determined that a payment fbr the otTicial's attendance al a private
concert not open to the general public was also a gifi subject to the Act's gift limit if paid for by the nonprofit
organization paying for the otTicial's travel and must be valued as an invitation-only event under Regulaiion
18946.2. (Fetlerighi Advise Letrer, No. A-}1--139.)
However, we have looked at several factors when determining whether a tour is indeed
informational material, including whether the tour will primarily convey information, whether
the tour is specifically designed for public officials, and whether the tour is formally structured to
convey information to the officials. Additionally, we consider whether the tour appears
extravagant in value or nature in relationship to the information conveyed. (Barker Advice
Letter, No. t-93-186.) In sum, a tour is informational material only if it is for the purpose of
assisting officials in the performance of their official duties. (See Federtgfti Advice Letter,
supra.)
According to the itinerary you have provided, the vast majority of the activities described
are tours of historical and cultural significance relating to Turkey and its various regions.
However, payments made for an official's tour do not fall within the exception to the Act's gift
limits for informational material merely because it can be said that the tour offers a historical or
cultural perspective of a country or region. For the most part, these tours appear to be
recreational in nature, and any relationship between the tours and your official duties with the
Encinitas City Council is tenuous at best. Thus, payments made for these activities (other than
travel payments for your transportation, lodging, or subsistence) are gifts subject to the $420 gift
limit, as well as reporting. As examples, payments made for admission to a museum or other
historical site, a tour guide, or other similar recreational activities are gifts subject to the $420
gift limit barring additional facts more closely tying the payments to your duties as a city council
member.
Nonetheless, your planned activities while in Turkey also include visits to educational
institutions, hospitals, and media facilities. These activities appear to serve a purpose more
closely tied to your duties as a city council member, and are not the types of activities commonly
associated with a recreational tourist. Thus, payments relating to these activities are exempt
from the Act's gift provisions as informational material under Regulation LBg42.
Organized Meals
The itinerary provided lists numerous organized meals during the course of the trip.6
While the Act's exception for travel paid for by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization covers
subsistence such as meals and beverages, payments made for activities arranged in coordination
with a meal do not fall within the exception for travel paid for by a nonprofit organization merely
because the activities occur simultaneously with the meal. For example, an invitation-only euer,i
such as a banquet, party, gala, celebration, or other similar ftrnction must be valued as provided
for by Regulation 18946.2, which states the following in pertinent part:
6 You have identified at least one meal that will be provided in the home of a local Turkish family. We
note that Regulation 18942(a)(7) provides an exception to the Act's gifi rules fbr..home hospitality,.'which is
defined as "the cost of hospitality (including fbod, beverages, or occasional lodging) provided to an official by an
individual in the individual's home when the individual is present" so long as the costs are not paid for by another
person, no person deducts the costs as a business expense on any government tax return, and there is no
understanding between the individual extending the hospitality and another person that compensation received by
the individual from the person includes a portion to be utilized to provide gitts of hospitality.


File No. A-lO-077
"(b) lnvitation-Only Events. ...when an official or candidate
attends an invitation-only event such as a banquet, party, gala, celebration,
or other similar ftrnction, other than a nonprofit or political ftrndraiser as
set forth in Regulation 18946.4, the value received is the official's or
candidate's pro rata share of the cost of the event, plus the value of any
specific item that is presented to the official or candidate at the event.
"(c) Pro rata share of the cost of the event. The term .pro rata
share of the cost of the event' means the cost of all food and beverages,
rent of the facilities, decorations, entertainment, and all other costs
associated with the event, divided by the number of acceptances or the
number of attendees."
Pursuant to Regulation 18946.2. if any individual or entity other than one as
specified in Section 89506(aX2) pays for an invitation-only event, the pro rata cost of the
event is a gift to you and subject to the $420 gift limit. Nonetheless, subsistence such as
a meal and beverages provided at an invitation-only event paid for by a 501(c)(3)
nonprofit organization or other individual or entity specified in Section 89506(aX2),
while in Turkey, are not subject to the Act's $420 gift limit. Thus, for an invitation-only
event paid for by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization, only the remainder of the pro-rata
share of the cost of the event excluding the value of the meal and beverages is subject to
the $420 gift limit. However, even if a meal and beverages are providedly a 501(c)(3)
nonprofit organization or other individual or entity specified in Section 89506(a)(2), your
full pro-rata share of the event must be reported and may potentially disqualify you fiom
taking part in decisions affecting the source of the payment.
Source of Gift
You have indicated that payments for the "cultural exchange" will come from the
Pacifica Institute's donors and sponsors. Thus, you must determine whether the source of any
particular payment is the Pacifica Institute or the donor or sponsor making a payment to the
Pacifica Institute for the event. Recently adopted Regulation 18945 prouid"r the Commission's
rule for determining the source of a gift and provides the following, in pertinent part:
"(a) General Rule. A person is the source of a gift if the person makes a
gift to an official and is not acting as an intermediary.
"(b) Gifts through an Intermediary.
"(l) A person is the source of a gift, and a third party is an intermediary of
the gift under Sections 87210 or 87313, if the person makes a payment to the third
party and the payment is used directly or indirectly by the third party to make a
gift to an official under any of the following conclitions:


File No. A-lO-077
"(A) The person directs and controls the payment at the time it is used by
the third party to make a gift to the official.
"(B) The person and third party have agreed that the payment rvill be used
by the third party to make a gift to the official.
"(C) The person identifie
beneficiary of the payment prior
official.
s the official to the third party as the intended
to the third party making the payment to the
"(D) The third party identifies the official to the person as the intended
beneficiary of the payment prior to the person making the payment to rhe third
party.
"(E) The person knows or has reason to know that the sole or primary
purpose of the payment is to make gifts to officials. 
r
"(F) The official or the official's agent solicits the payment from the
person to the third party for the purpose of making a gift to the official."
Notwithstanding the rule for determining the source of a gift in Regulation 1g945(b)(2),
subdivision (d) provides:
"(d) Presumption of Source by Officials. An official may presllme that the
person delivering the gift or, if the gift is offered but has not been delivered, the
person offering the gift to him or her is the source of the gift unless any of the
following apply:
"(1) The person delivering or offering the gift discloses to the official the
actual source of the gift.
"(2) It is clear from the surrounding circumstances at the time the gift is
delivered or offered that the person delivering or offering the gift is not the actual
soLlrce of the gift.
"(3) The official solicits a payment pursuant to subdivision (bXlXF) and
receives or is offered a gift responsive to the solicitation within l2 months of
making the solicitation.
While you have indicated that payments for the "cultural exchange" will be made by the
donors and sponsors to the Pacifica [nstitute, you have not provided any-indication that the
payments made to the Pacitica Institute were in any way earmarked foian oflicial. Signiticantly,
the Pacifica Institttte has extended invitations to the "cultural exchange" to faith comrrurnity
leaders and academicians, as well as govemment officials. From these facts alone, you have no
reason to suspect that that the Pacifica Institute is not the actual sollrce of the gift. provided that
you did not solicit payments to the Pacifica Institute and barring additional information
indicating that the Pacifica Institute is acting as an intermediary for the payments pursuant to
Regulation 18945(b), you may assume the Pacifica Institute is the source of any payments for the
"cultural exchange."
lf you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (9L6) 322-5660.

QUESTION: Are payments for your participation in a "cultural exchange" in [stanbul, Turkey,
including but not limited to your transportation while in Turkey, iccommodations, meals, and
museum visits, prohibited gifts under the Act?

CONCLUSION: Payments for your travel, including your transportation while in Turkey and related
lodging and subsistence, are gifts under the Act and must be reported on your annual statement
of economic interest. As reportable gifts, these payments may also requiie you to disqualify
yourself from governmental decisions affecting the source of the gifts. Notwithstanding the fact
that these payments are reportable, if the payments are made by the Pacifica Institute, o SOt("X:)
nonprofit organization, they are not subject to the Act's $420 gift limit.
Apart from payments for travel such as transportation and related lodging and
subsistence, payments for your activities while in Turkey may also be gifts urd". the Act.
of the r'-air political practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through ltt997 of Tirle 2 of thi Calitirrnia Code of Regulati.ns. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-lO-077
However, these payments must be examined on a case-by-case basis under the Act's applicable
gift mles. For activities such as tours, payments are reportable gifts and subject to the Act's
$420 gift limit unless the tour is considered informational material as discusied below.
Additionally, your pro-rata share of the cost of an invitation-only event is reportable and subject
to the $420 gift limit, except that payments for subsistence such as food and beverages provided
at the event are not counted towards the gift limit if paid for by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit
organization or other individual or entity specified in Section g9506(a)(2).

QUESTION Are payments for your participation in a "cultural exchange" in [stanbul, Turkey, including but not limited to your transportation while in Turkey, iccommodations, meals, and museum visits, prohibited gifts under the Act? FACTS You are a city council member for the City of Encinitas and have been invited by the Pacifica Institute San Diego Branch, a nonprofit organization under Section 501(cX3) oi the Internal Revenue Code, to a "cultural exchange" event in Istanbul, Turkey.
